description: GitLab CI/CD OIDC subjects against AWS IAM role trust-policy conditions (StringEquals / StringLike). Second issuer. GitLab sub default = project_path:{group}/{project}:ref_type:{branch|tag}:ref:{short_ref}; the immutable form (project_id-led) requires reconfiguring ci_id_token_sub_claim_components. AWS condition key is gitlab.com:sub; the matcher is host-agnostic and matches the raw sub string, so claim stays 'sub'. GitLab immutable ids (project_id), ref_protected, and pipeline_source are SEPARATE claims, not in the sub; the 0.2.0 aws-all tranche composes them with the sub into evaluated multi-key condition blocks, encoding the fixes the 0.1.x judgment prose could only recommend. All GitLab claim values used here are JSON STRINGS in the token ("20", "true", "push" -- the docs' example payload distinguishes them from bare numbers like runner_id: 1). The nine GitLab-specific AWS condition keys are GitLab.com-SaaS-only ('You cannot use these condition keys with GitLab Dedicated or GitLab Self-Managed instances'); GitLab's own docs disagree on what Self-Managed/Dedicated retain (id_token_authentication: sub only; cloud_services/aws: sub and aud).

vectors.description: Default name-based sub pinned to one branch of one project, matched exactly. Tight on identity but name-based.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: Exact single-project single-branch pin, but project_path is mutable (rename/transfer/path-reuse) and branch protection lives in the separate ref_protected claim, which a single-key sub condition cannot require. Harden by pinning gitlab.com:project_id and gitlab.com:ref_protected=true as additional condition keys, or use the project_id-led sub.

vectors.description: Rename-proof sub after setting ci_id_token_sub_claim_components=[project_id,ref_type,ref]. No mutable path anywhere in the condition. The GitLab analogue of a GitHub immutable-subject exact pin.
vectors.subject: project_id:20:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: The sub is built on the immutable project_id (globally unique, constant for the project lifetime), so the pin survives rename/transfer/path-reuse without a separate condition key, and pins one branch. Requires the project to reconfigure ci_id_token_sub_claim_components.

vectors.description: Group-level wildcard on project_path. GitLab's documented group-wide sub filter; the analogue of the GitHub org-wide repo:org/* wildcard.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: Any project in mygroup (at any subgroup depth), any ref_type, any ref can assume the role, because IAM StringLike * is not path-aware and spans '/' and ':'. New projects created under the group inherit trust immediately.

vectors.description: A deeply nested subgroup project still matches project_path:mygroup/* because * spans '/'. Shows the wildcard's unbounded reach across subgroup depth.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/subgroup/deepproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: project_path carries the whole group/subgroup/project chain, and StringLike * is not path-aware, so mygroup/* admits every project at any nesting depth under mygroup. Breadth is inferred from the documented nested-path format composed with AWS non-path-aware * semantics.

vectors.description: The same project_path:mygroup/* string is inert under StringEquals: * is a literal character, so it matches only the literal string, never a real subject. The operator, not the string, decides.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/otherproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

vectors.description: Exact pin to a tag. ref_type is 'tag' and ref is the short tag name. Tags are movable and unprotected by default.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:tag:ref:v1.0.0

judgment.reason: Pins one tag of one project, but tags are movable and, unless protected, anyone with push access can create or repoint a tag named v1.0.0 and mint this subject. A single-key sub condition cannot require ref_protected=true. Prefer a protected tag plus a gitlab.com:ref_protected=true condition key.

vectors.description: Wildcarding ref_type collapses the branch/tag distinction. A policy meant for the main branch is satisfied by a TAG named main. Distinct over-permission class because GitLab (unlike GitHub) separates ref_type from ref.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:tag:ref:main

judgment.reason: ref_type:* admits both a branch and a tag named main, so an attacker who can create a tag named main (weaker than protected-branch push) mints a matching subject. Inferred from the documented ref_type value set {branch,tag} composed with AWS StringLike * spanning the ref_type segment. Pin ref_type explicitly to branch.

vectors.description: Project pinned, but ref:* admits any branch, including an attacker-pushed feature branch. No ref_protected guard is expressible in a single-key sub condition.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:feature-x

judgment.reason: Any branch of the project satisfies the condition, so a Developer (or anyone who can push a branch) runs a pipeline that assumes the role. ref_protected is a separate claim; add a gitlab.com:ref_protected=true condition key and narrow ref.

vectors.description: GitLab's sub has NO merge_request marker (contrast GitHub's repo:owner/repo:pull_request sub). A merge-request pipeline's sub is just ref_type:branch:ref:<source-branch>, so a ref:* policy silently admits MR-triggered runs and you cannot exclude them via the sub alone.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:contributor-mr-branch

judgment.reason: The sub encodes only project_path/ref_type/ref, with no pipeline-source marker, so an MR pipeline on a source branch is indistinguishable from a normal branch push and is admitted by ref:*. Untrusted contributor code under review can assume the role. Gate on the separate pipeline_source claim (e.g. require 'push') and pin the branch; the sub alone is insufficient.

vectors.description: The reclaimed-path token evaluated against the victim's stale trust policy: the claims model a DIFFERENT project (project_id 999) now occupying mygroup/myproject and minting a byte-identical path-based sub, which the exact StringEquals condition cannot distinguish from the original. Mitigated upstream, not fixed in the policy: 'As of June 1, 2026, GitLab has deployed a platform-side update that prevents new CI ID tokens from being issued for project paths that previously issued tokens' (AWS WIF condition-keys note, GitLab.com tab). In GitLab's source the burn covers every path-vacating flow -- delete, rename, AND transfer (commit f7335ef7e 'Block CI ID token issuance on reclaimed namespace paths'; original project exempt; path-based subs only) -- but it is NOT retroactive for paths vacated before the rollout, and GitLab's own user docs do not describe the blocking. The GitLab cybersquat/path-reuse analogue of the GitHub rename vector.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: project_path is mutable and paths are reusable after rename/transfer/delete, so a different project's token (project_id 999 here) satisfies the condition. GitLab's platform-side burn closes the common reclaim path going forward, but it is non-retroactive, absent on self-managed instances that predate the change, and invisible to the policy: the trust policy itself still pins nothing immutable. Add a gitlab.com:project_id (and/or namespace_id) condition key -- 'AWS and GitLab recommend including conditions on stable, unique identifiers' -- or move to the project_id-led sub (see gitlab-aws-immutable-projectid-sub-stringequals).

vectors.description: GitLab's documented example trust policy as an evaluated block: 'The following example trust policy uses sub together with namespace_id and project_id to pin trust to a specific group, project, and branch on GitLab.com', condition values verbatim from the doc (all JSON strings). The doc notes project_id 'is globally unique and remains the same for the entire lifetime of the project, including across group renames, project renames, and project transfers', while namespace_id changes on transfer. Observational note: the doc's example carries no aud condition key.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: The full documented hardening: exact branch-scoped sub AND the immutable project_id AND the namespace_id, so neither a path rename/reuse nor a look-alike project in another group satisfies the block. This is the evaluated form of the fix the path-reuse vector recommends.

vectors.description: The path-reuse attack against the HARDENED policy: the same squatter token that satisfies the sub-only condition in gitlab-aws-path-reuse-no-projectid (byte-identical path-based sub, different project ids) fails the documented triple pin, because the id keys are ANDed with the sub and the squatter cannot mint the original's project_id.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: The pin works policy-side, independent of GitLab's platform-side path burn: the reclaimed path reproduces the sub string but not the immutable ids, and 'All context keys in a condition element block must resolve to true to invoke the desired Allow or Deny effect.'

vectors.description: AWS's documented GitLab example as an evaluated block: 'The following example trust policy uses the namespace_id claim together with sub to limit access to a specific GitLab.com project and branch', values verbatim (namespace_id is the JSON string '12345'). Two keys, not three: unlike GitLab's own example it does not pin project_id, which is the id that 'remains the same for the entire lifetime of the project' across transfers.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: Group pinned via namespace_id AND exact path-based sub -- materially stronger than the sub alone, but the project itself is still identified only by its mutable path: a path vacated and reclaimed WITHIN the same group keeps namespace_id 12345 and reproduces the sub (inference from the documented claim set). GitLab's fuller documented example additionally pins project_id, the identifier stable across renames and transfers; prefer the triple pin.

vectors.description: The ref_protected guard the 0.1.x vectors could only recommend, evaluated: exact branch sub AND gitlab.com:ref_protected compared to the STRING 'true' ('The value is true if protected, false otherwise'; the token claim is a JSON string -- the docs' example payload shows "ref_protected": "false" in quotes, unlike the bare number runner_id: 1).
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: The protection guard now holds mechanically -- only a run on a protected ref satisfies the block -- but the identity half is still the mutable project_path. Pair the guard with a project_id pin (see gitlab-aws-all-docs-triple-pin) to close the rename/path-reuse residual.

vectors.description: Protection is STATE, not name: the same branch name mints ref_protected 'false' when its protection rule is absent or removed, and the guard catches what the sub string cannot -- the sub here is byte-identical to the matching sibling vector, only the protection claim differs ('false' is the docs' own example payload value).
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: The guard fails closed -- a run on the right branch in the wrong protection state is refused, which a sub-only condition can never express -- but the condition being demonstrated is the same name-based block graded caution in gitlab-aws-all-refprotected-guard-match: the identity half is still the mutable project_path, so pair the guard with a project_id pin.

vectors.description: The pipeline_source gate for the no-merge_request-marker problem (gitlab-aws-merge-request-source-branch-admitted): GitLab's sub has no MR marker and an MR pipeline's sub carries the SOURCE branch ref ('In a merge request pipeline, this is the source branch ref'), so a ref wildcard admits MR runs -- but the separate pipeline_source claim (JSON string; 'push' for Git push pipelines, 'merge_request_event' for merge request pipelines) is pinnable via gitlab.com:pipeline_source, whose documented example values include push and merge_request_event.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:contributor-mr-branch

judgment.reason: The gate closes the MR event class -- the MR-triggered token fails the pipeline_source key even though its source-branch sub sails through the ref wildcard -- but every PUSH pipeline on every branch still satisfies the block, and a developer pushing a branch triggers exactly a push pipeline: the any-developer-branch breadth graded dangerous in gitlab-aws-any-branch-no-refprotected survives the gate untouched. Narrow the ref and add ref_protected; pipeline_source alone changes which event, not who.
